gaps.title: init / switch -c bootstrap leaves the substrate config-less, with no op-driven path to a custom-vocabulary config
gaps.description: initProject (the context-init op) and switchAndCreate (/context switch -c) scaffold the substrate dir + schemas/ subdir but write no config.json. The only op that originates a config is acceptCanonicalConfig/accept-all, which adopts the entire packaged conception (all-or-nothing, never-clobber). amendConfigEntry throws 'no config.json' when the config is absent, so it cannot bootstrap one. Net: a user who wants a substrate with a custom vocabulary rather than the full packaged catalog has no op-driven path and must hand-author config.json, falling off the tool surface.

evidence.reference: initProject scaffolds dirs + pointer only; writes no config.json
switchAndCreate flips pointer + scaffolds dirs only; writes no config.json
amendConfigEntry throws 'no config.json' when config absent — cannot originate a config
adoptConception never-clobber guard if(existing) + whole-catalog adoption — only config-originating op, all-or-nothing
Live dogfooding state: pointer switched to using-pi-context/, which holds only an empty schemas/ subdir and no config.json — the broken state with no ops path forward

gaps.impact: Custom-vocabulary substrate creation is impossible through the tool surface; a dogfooded /context switch -c lands the user on a config-less substrate that no op can advance (accept-all would impose the whole catalog; amend-config refuses without a config).
gaps.proposed_resolution: init + switch -c write a minimal schema-valid skeleton config (schema_version + empty block_kinds + minted substrate_id) via a shared never-clobber writeSkeletonConfig helper; accept-all becomes skeleton-aware (overwrite when absent OR isSkeletonConfig, never-clobber a populated config); deriveBootstrapState gains a 'skeleton' state ordered no-pointer -> no-config -> skeleton -> not-installed -> ready; onward-guidance messages name the two paths (accept-all OR amend/edit).

gaps.title: No substrate clone/import: cross-project substrate reuse has no op-driven path and substrate identity carries no provenance
gaps.description: substrate_id is content-addressed, but the project-root registry (.pi-context-registry.json) is a flat id -> { dir, aliases } map with no origin/provenance. Item-level cross-substrate import exists (promoteItem mints a fresh content-addressed identity + records item_derived_from_item lineage), but there is no substrate-level analogue. Bringing a pi-context substrate in from another project therefore requires manual file copy + a manual registry edit, and yields a false substrate_id_unregistered drift error because the SoT-drift invariant cannot distinguish a native substrate whose registry entry was lost from a legitimately imported foreign one.

evidence.reference: registry entry shape is { dir, aliases } only — no origin/provenance field
SoT-drift invariant: an unregistered config.substrate_id is a single error code with no native-vs-foreign discrimination
promoteItem provides item-level cross-substrate import with item_derived_from_item lineage + fresh content-addressed identity; no substrate-level counterpart exists
the only registerSubstrate call sites record id+dir+aliases, no origin — so an imported substrate's lineage is unrecorded

gaps.impact: No tool-driven cross-project substrate reuse; imported substrates carry no lineage to their origin; the SoT-drift invariant false-positives on legitimate imports; and the local switch-register reconciliation fix cannot tell a native-lost-registry substrate from a foreign import, so registering blindly on switch would silently absorb a foreign or colliding id.
gaps.proposed_resolution: Lift substrate identity to the content-addressed + lineage model items already use: a substrate-level substrate_derived_from_substrate relation, provenance-bearing registry entries (minted-here vs derived-from), and a cloneSubstrate op (fork semantics: copy content into a new dir, mint a fresh substrate_id, record the derived-from edge + origin) surfaced as a dual-surface pi-context clone (library + Pi tool + CLI + orchestrator script). Preserves the one-id<->one-dir SoT invariant. See DEC-0002.

gaps.title: switch / switch -c do not reconcile the active substrate's identity into the registry, yielding a false substrate_id_unregistered
gaps.description: Switching the active pointer to a substrate whose config.json carries a substrate_id absent from this project's .pi-context-registry.json produces a false substrate_id_unregistered error from the SoT-drift invariant, though nothing is functionally broken. Registration happens only in writeSkeletonConfig (fresh write) and adoptConception (accept-all); switchToExisting only checks config presence then flips the pointer, and switchAndCreate's writeSkeletonConfig never-clobber skips the write (and its register) when a config already exists. So neither switch path records the now-active substrate's identity.

evidence.reference: switchToExisting checks config.json exists + flipBootstrapPointer; never calls registerSubstrate
the only registerSubstrate call sites — writeSkeletonConfig fresh write + adoptConception
SoT-drift invariant emits substrate_id_unregistered when config.substrate_id has no registry entry

gaps.impact: A valid switch to an unregistered substrate (copied-in, hand-authored, or surviving a registry reset) fails context-validate with a false error; the remedy today is a manual registerSubstrate or a clobber-guarded accept-all.
gaps.proposed_resolution: switch paths register config.substrate_id -> active dir when the id is absent from the registry (functionally safe: absent => no collision; a present-but-different-dir entry remains the substrate_id_registry_mismatch error). Origin/provenance recording for imported substrates defers to DEC-0002. See DEC-0003.

gaps.title: pi-context has no structure-aware git merge driver — substrate files fall through to git's line-based merge, which mangles them
gaps.description: Substrate state lives in git-tracked files (<block>.json, relations.json, config.json, schemas, objects/). When two branches carrying the same substrate are merged, git's default line-based merge diffs the JSON text, mangling arrays and producing structurally invalid or semantically wrong states. pi-context registers no custom git merge driver, so substrate files fall through to that line merge. The gap: a custom git merge driver (registered via .gitattributes) that, given git's three versions (%O ancestor / %A ours / %B theirs), performs a structure-aware per-file 3-way merge — matching items by stable id/oid rather than array index, merging edges as a set, merging ordered ref-fields via the convergent ordered-sequence field-type (FGAP-005), and merging config/schema — and writes a data-model-valid result; PLUS a post-merge pass for what a per-file driver structurally cannot do: cross-block referential integrity (e.g. an edge whose endpoint item was deleted in another file) and whole-substrate invariant + lens revalidation. Git owns branches, commits, merge-base, refs, and invocation; pi-context owns only the structure-aware reconciliation.

evidence.reference: substrate writes are whole-item upsert; nothing registers a git merge driver, so <block>.json / relations.json merge via git's line-based default, which mangles JSON arrays and yields structurally invalid states
no merge=<driver> attribute declared for substrate files (<block>.json / relations.json / config.json) — they get git's default line merge
content-addressed objects/ are distinct hash-named files (equal hash == equal content), so git's directory merge unions them without conflict — no driver needed there

gaps.impact: Merging two branches that both edited the same substrate corrupts it: concurrent array reorders / inserts produce invalid or wrong JSON, and cross-block referential integrity + declared invariants are never re-checked after a merge. Same-repo collaborative editing on a pi-context substrate is unsafe under normal git workflows.
gaps.proposed_resolution: Ship a git merge driver (.gitattributes merge=pi-context + a [merge "pi-context"] driver entry) performing per-file structure-aware 3-way merge of substrate files from git's %O/%A/%B (items by id/oid, edge-set merge, ordered ref-fields via FGAP-005, config/schema), writing a data-model-valid result; plus a post-merge finalize/validate hook (git post-merge or a merge-finalize command) for cross-block referential integrity + invariant/lens revalidation, emitting a typed conflict set for what cannot auto-resolve. Branches, merge-base, commit lineage, refs, and cherry-pick stay git's. See DEC-0004.

gaps.title: Ordered collections of item-refs need a convergent sequence field-kind — an array index cannot merge concurrent insert / delete / move
gaps.description: Order is a plain ordered array of refs on the owning item (e.g. a fragment_refs / phase-order / step-list field), authoritative by design. Single-writer insert/reorder works: splice the array, one atomic item write. But the array carries no merge semantics: two divergent versions that each reorder it 3-way-merge to a conflict (git) or whole-array last-writer-wins (item upsert), losing one side's ordering. The gap: when two divergent substrate versions each insert, delete, and/or move elements in the same ordered collection, the substrate must merge them into one deterministic, replica-independent sequence containing every insert, with each element retaining a single identity and a single position, and concurrently-inserted runs kept coherent rather than interleaved. An array index cannot carry this, because position is a value two branches generate independently and must reconcile. The capability is latent until the structure-aware git merge driver (FGAP-004) exists, which is the host that invokes this field-level merge on git's three versions of the owning block file.

evidence.reference: ordering today is a plain ordered array of refs on the owning item (authoritative by design); position is an array index two branches generate independently and cannot reconcile
item upsert is whole-array last-writer-wins and git line-merge mangles arrays; neither merges concurrent insert/delete/move on an ordered ref-array — one side's ordering is lost

gaps.impact: Ordered collections of item-refs (roadmaps, milestone sequences, fragment composition, step lists) in substrates branched and merged across operators / sessions / branches cannot converge: concurrent reordering on divergent versions conflicts or silently drops an ordering.
gaps.proposed_resolution: A declared sequence-field kind whose elements carry unique, densely-orderable, immutable position identifiers minted relative to neighbors at insert time (non-interleaving Fugue-class sequence CRDT); move as a first-class op via a position register resolved by hybrid logical clock (Kleppmann move-CRDT) with stable element identity; HLC for replica-independent same-gap ordering + move resolution; a field-aware merge driver composing the per-field CRDT op-sets/states; a sort-on-read projection emitting a plain ordered ref-array (and translating array-position hand-edits back to CRDT ops); authoritative CRDT state in the owning <block>.json, projection + merge driver in block-api. Plugs into the substrate merge driver (FGAP-004) as a field-kind. See DEC-0005.

gaps.title: relations.json has no scoped remove/replace edge op — edge hygiene is append-only through the tool surface
gaps.description: append-relation is a full dual-surface op (library appendRelation + Pi tool + orchestrator script + CLI). There is no counterpart to remove or replace a single edge. Whole-file writeRelations exists (library only, consumed by rename-canonical-id) but there is no scoped remove-relation primitive, no Pi tool, and no CLI op. So a mis-filed edge (wrong direction / type / endpoint — e.g. the TASK-004/TASK-005 dependency just corrected) cannot be fixed through the operator/agent tool surface; only ad-hoc loadRelations+filter+writeRelations library code can, which harness-confined in-pi agents (registered Pi tools only) cannot run.

evidence.reference: append-relation is dual-surface (library appendRelation + Pi tool + CLI + script); no remove/replace counterpart exists anywhere
writeRelations is a whole-file replace (library only, used by rename-canonical-id); grep for removeRelation/deleteRelation/pruneRelation returns none — no scoped single-edge removal
the backwards TASK-005->TASK-004 edge was removable only via orchestrator-side loadRelations+filter+writeRelations (5621c2a), unavailable to cli-only or in-pi-tool-only consumers

gaps.impact: Edges are append-only through tools; mis-directions, typos, and superseded edges accumulate uncorrectably for cli-only and harness-confined consumers, who would need privileged library access to fix them — breaking the dual-surface symmetry append-relation otherwise has.
gaps.proposed_resolution: A scoped remove-relation (and replace-relation) as a dual-surface unit mirroring append-relation: library removeRelation(cwd, {parent, child, relation_type}) (idempotent whole-file rewrite minus the matched edge) + Pi tool + CLI op + orchestrator script; run context-validate after for referential integrity. replace-relation = remove+append in one call for direction/type corrections.

gaps.title: Ordering relation_type names read opposite to the parent/child convention (task_depends_on_task footgun) — backwards edges file silently
gaps.description: relation_type canonical_ids use source_verb_target naming, so task_depends_on_task reads as parent(source) depends_on child(target). But the closure-table convention + the current-state deriver treat parent as the PREREQUISITE and child as the DEPENDENT — the child is blocked until the parent completes (context-sdk.ts:667-722). Name and stored direction are therefore opposite: the name implies the parent is the depender; the system treats the parent as the depended-upon. append-relation does no semantic direction check, so a backwards ordering edge is accepted silently and only surfaces in derived state — it just produced a backwards TASK-004/TASK-005 dependency, corrected at 5621c2a.

evidence.reference: edge-direction contract: {parent: D, child: T} => T blocked until D completed (parent=prerequisite, child=dependent) — opposite to the source_verb_target name reading
relation_type task_depends_on_task display_name 'depends on task', category ordering — name reads 'parent depends on child' while the deriver treats parent as prerequisite
the corrected dependency edge (5621c2a): original parent=TASK-005/child=TASK-004 was filed per the name, wrong per the deriver

gaps.impact: Any ordering relation whose verb implies the parent is the actor while the deriver treats parent as the prerequisite is easy to mis-direct; append-time silence means the error is invisible until topo/blocked derivation. Affects dependency-ordering correctness across tasks and any similarly-shaped ordering relation (e.g. phase_depends_on).
gaps.proposed_resolution: Carry explicit endpoint-role metadata on the relation_type (which endpoint is the prerequisite vs the dependent) and have appendRelation / append-relation validate or normalize an ordering edge's direction against it at write time, so name and stored direction cannot diverge and a backwards edge is rejected (or auto-oriented) at append rather than discovered in derived state. Extends the existing relation-type endpoint-kind metadata; the deriver already consumes the contract, so the fix makes the WRITE path enforce the same contract.

gaps.title: Op-execution contract carries no DispatchContext channel — every cli / in-pi write op is attestation-blind
gaps.description: OpDefinition.run(cwd, params) has no DispatchContext channel (ops-registry.ts:104); registerAll invokes op.run(ctx.cwd, params), threading only ctx.cwd (:1519); the cli injects a writer only when an op's params schema declares a 'writer' property (cli.ts:292). So every write op whose schema omits 'writer' structurally cannot forward the library's ctx?: DispatchContext, and attestation stamping (stampItem, dispatch-context.ts:121-152) never runs. Ten write ops drop the ctx: append/update/remove-block-item, the nested trio, write-block, append-relation, plus amend-config and write-schema (which pass a literal undefined at the ctx slot). On attested-required schemas (framework-gaps / decisions / features require created_by + created_at) the cli filing fails AJV unless the caller hand-authors author fields. The same drop recurs in pi-workflows step-block.ts (six writers). The append-block-item ctx-drop is the seed instance.

evidence.reference: OpDefinition.run(cwd, params) — no DispatchContext channel in the contract (verified)
registerAll invokes op.run(ctx.cwd, params) — pi ExtensionContext identity not threaded (verified)
injectWriter sets params.writer only if the op schema declares a 'writer' property (verified)
append-block-item calls appendToBlock(cwd, block, arrayKey, item) — ctx (4th arg) dropped (verified)
all six block-api writers called without ctx — same drop

gaps.impact: Every cli- or workflow-written item is unattested; the DispatchContext attestation model (created_by stamping; human/agent/monitor/workflow writer kinds) is silently bypassed; attested-required schemas reject cli filings. This is why filing has gone through the orchestrator scripts (which thread ctx), not the cli.
gaps.proposed_resolution: Thread a writer/DispatchContext through the op-execution contract itself (a ctx/writer channel on OpDefinition.run + registerAll + the cli writer-injection) so every write op stamps uniformly — rather than per-op schema smuggling (the channel only promote-item / write-schema-migration / context-switch use today, and which context-archive declares-but-ignores). See DEC-0006.

gaps.title: Op-registry does not cover the library write surface — whole capabilities unreachable from the cli / out-of-pi
gaps.description: Library write capabilities have no op-registry entry, so they are unreachable from pi-context-cli and in-pi tools: edge removal/replace (removeRelation does not exist in the library at all; writeRelations whole-replace has no op), upsertItemInBlock (atomic find-or-replace), writeConfig (whole-config), writeSkeletonConfig, writeSchema/updateSchema (unchecked write + mutator), registerSubstrate/writeRegistry (foreign-substrate registration), writeBootstrapPointer/flipBootstrapPointer (raw pointer), and bulk appendRelations. An operator restricted to the cli cannot correct a mis-filed edge, run an atomic upsert, or write a config/registry/pointer.

evidence.reference: writeRelations (whole-relations replace) + appendRelations (bulk) have no op; removeRelation does not exist — relations are append-only via ops
upsertItemInBlock atomic find-or-replace — no op (used only by pi-behavior-monitors directly)
writeSchema/updateSchema — no op (only write-schema -> writeSchemaChecked)
writeRegistry/registerSubstrate — no op; foreign-substrate registration unreachable from cli
writeBootstrapPointer/flipBootstrapPointer — no op

gaps.impact: The cli is not a faithful interface to the library write surface; whole categories of substrate operation are out-of-pi-unreachable. FGAP-006 (no edge-removal op) is a specific instance, with the sub-layer that the removeRelation library primitive is itself missing.
gaps.proposed_resolution: A systematic op per library write capability as a dual-surface unit (library + Pi tool/op + CLI + orchestrator script): add the missing removeRelation library primitive + a remove-relation / replace-relation op (closing FGAP-006), and ops for writeRelations / upsertItemInBlock / writeConfig / writeSchema / registerSubstrate / bootstrap-pointer writers / bulk appendRelations as the surface warrants. See DEC-0006.

gaps.title: No enforced parity across the library / op-registry / orchestrator-script surfaces — they drift silently
gaps.description: The dual-surface principle ('library + tool + script as a unit') is unenforced. The orchestrator scripts build a DispatchContext and forward ctx + optional params (dryRun / ordinal) that the ops drop; the op-registry omits library write fns entirely; nothing catches the divergence at build/test. So projection loss (FGAP-008) and coverage gaps (FGAP-009) accrue silently — e.g. appendToBlock is called WITH ctx by file-block-item.ts:273 and pi-behavior-monitors:1612 but WITHOUT ctx by op append-block-item (ops-registry.ts:174) and pi-workflows step-block.ts:203.

evidence.reference: builds DispatchContext + passes ctx to appendToBlock — op append-block-item drops it
forwards ordinal + ctx to appendRelationByRef — the op forwards ordinal but drops ctx
no test asserts op-registry coverage of the library write surface or ctx/param forwarding parity

gaps.impact: FGAP-008 and FGAP-009 recur on every new library writer or op without a guard; the three surfaces silently diverge; attestation correctness and cli completeness can regress undetected.
gaps.proposed_resolution: CLOSED at dc7f514 by scripts/parity-check.ts — the source-intrinsic build gate (husky pre-commit + CI) that AST-enumerates library writers, classifies each against OP_COVERAGE_RULE, fails fatally on an unclassified writer or a silent ctx drop, and reports dual-surface optional-param mismatches non-fatally. The dual-surface unit is now self-policing: a new library writer or op that drops ctx fails the build. The 5 non-fatal dryRun script/op asymmetries the gate now surfaces are tracked as FGAP-012.

gaps.title: No release/version vocabulary — releases and changelog grouping cannot be planned or tracked as first-class substrate items
gaps.description: The substrate (and the packaged conception) has no release/version block kind, no version field, no target/date, and no ships-in-release relation. A release plan can only be modeled by overloading phase (task_positioned_in_phase) or story (story_contains_task) plus a DEC; nothing represents a release as a first-class object binding a version tag + the set of completed dev-items (closed tasks/gaps) + their changelog lines. Changelog text lives in per-package CHANGELOG.md, enforced only at commit-time by the changelog guard, disconnected from the substrate's dev-item lifecycle. So there is no substrate-native way to plan 'release N ships these items' in advance, nor to verify that a release reflects the organized set of done dev-items.

evidence.reference: block_kinds has no release/version/milestone kind; planning relation_types are phase/story/work-order groupings only (task_positioned_in_phase, story_contains_task), none binding a version or release
the packaged conception ships no release/version block kind — the gap is in the catalog, not just this substrate
version bump + changelog stamping operate on CHANGELOG.md ([Unreleased]) at release time, disconnected from substrate task/gap closure
changelog forward-discipline is a manual practice; no substrate linkage between closed dev-items and changelog entries / releases

gaps.impact: Releases are planned and tracked ad-hoc (overloaded phase/story or a loose DEC); there is no first-class binding of version -> completed dev-items -> changelog lines, so the project risks losing the thread between organized dev work done and what shipped in which release.
gaps.proposed_resolution: A release/version vocabulary: a 'release' block kind (id, version, target/date, status planned|released, summary) + a relation binding closed dev-items to a release (e.g. release_contains_item / task_shipped_in_release / gap_closed_in_release) + optionally a milestone/roadmap instantiation; the release item carries or references the changelog lines for its items, so a release is the organized set of done dev-items with their changelog text — planned in advance and verifiable. Dual-surface (schema + conception registration + ops).

gaps.title: 5 relation/upsert ops omit the --dry-run their orchestrator-script siblings offer (dual-surface optional-param asymmetry)
gaps.description: The op-coverage parity gate (FGAP-010, landed dc7f514) reports, non-fatally, that 5 ops — append-relation, remove-relation, replace-relation, append-relations, upsert-block-item — do not declare a 'dryRun' parameter in their typebox parameters schema, while their orchestrator-script siblings under scripts/orchestrator/ DO parse a '--dry-run' flag. So a no-write preview is reachable out-of-pi via the script but not in-pi via the op or the CLI. This is distinct from amend-config / write-schema / promote-item, whose op schemas DO declare dryRun (those were the prior detector's false-positives, now corrected). Secondary, separate concern tracked here: the parity-check divergence detector itself carries two dormant edges the re-audit flagged — (a) opDeclaresParam reads only top-level schema .properties, so an op expressed via allOf/$ref/nested would yield a false divergence; (b) scriptParsesFlag matches a quote-hugged flag literal anywhere in the script text, so a print-only literal (a flag named in help text but not parsed) would false-match. Neither triggers on the current tree (all ops use flat Type.Object; no script prints a bare flag literal).

evidence.reference: parses --dry-run; the append-relation op schema declares ordinal but not dryRun
parses --dry-run; the remove-relation op schema declares no optional flags
parses --dry-run; the replace-relation op schema declares ordinal but not dryRun
parses --dry-run; the append-relations op schema declares no top-level dryRun
parses --dry-run; the upsert-block-item op schema declares idField but not dryRun

gaps.impact: Dry-run preview asymmetry between the in-pi op / CLI surface and the out-of-pi orchestrator scripts for edge mutation and atomic upsert: an in-pi caller cannot preview these five mutations the way the scripts can. Minor and advisory rather than a correctness defect, but it is exactly the dual-surface drift the parity gate exists to surface, so leaving it unaddressed lets the surfaces diverge further.
gaps.proposed_resolution: CLOSED across TASK-010 (8fcf02d, the 4 relation ops) + TASK-011 (4b72b86, upsert-block-item). The 5 relation/upsert ops now share ONE dryRun preview with their orchestrator scripts (the byRef/upsert library fns gained opts?:{dryRun} that validate the prospective + return without writing; ops declare/thread dryRun; scripts delegate). The parity-check gate's dual-surface divergence report is now at ZERO. Also tracked from this arc: FGAP-016 (pre-existing truncateHead measurement nuance, P3, non-leak).

gaps.title: CLI --json double-encodes structured op output (output is a stringified JSON string, not a JSON value)
gaps.description: Surfaced by dogfooding the CLI. Under --json the envelope at packages/pi-context-cli/src/cli.ts:487 emits {ok, op, output: text} where text is the op run() return. OpDefinition.run returns a string, and roughly 27 data-returning ops produce JSON as a string (via JSON.stringify or serializeForRead().content). So the output field under --json is a stringified JSON string, forcing consumers to double-parse: JSON.parse(JSON.parse(stdout).output). The roughly 21 prose-returning ops are unaffected. No test asserts the --json output shape, so the double-encode was never caught. Agent-investigated for root cause, shape, and reproducible conditions before filing.

evidence.reference: envelope wraps the op string return; a JSON-returning op double-encodes under --json
resolve-item-by-id / read-block-item / read-config run() return JSON.stringify(...) or serializeForRead().content, i.e. a string

gaps.impact: A machine consumer of --json must double-parse every structured op, which defeats the point of --json as machine-parseable output. The CLI surface the cli arc built is harder to consume programmatically and the inconsistency is silent (no test locks the shape).
gaps.proposed_resolution: CLOSED at 75f2611 (TASK-012). Data ops return a structured OpResult (string | {json} | {read}); the CLI --json envelope emits output as a JSON value, not a stringified-JSON string. Default + in-pi Pi-tool text route through renderOpResultText, byte-identical to each op prior run() text. Verified by contract tests + runtime demo (resolve-item-by-id --json output is an object, single-parse). The deeper cap-bypass the fix exposed is closed by FGAP-015 (same commit).

gaps.title: complete-task op is unusable against catalog substrates: reads verification fields the canonical schema does not declare (incomplete edge-migration refactor)
gaps.description: Surfaced when deciding how to complete TASK-008. completeTask (packages/pi-context/src/context-sdk.ts:2172) requires verification.target and verification.target_type === task, but neither the canonical samples verification schema nor the active .context schema declares those fields. Commit c08f93b intentionally removed target and target_type from the verification schema in favor of a verification_verifies_item closure-table edge, but completeTask was never updated to the edge-based link. The schemas are permissive (additionalProperties absent), so the op fails at runtime rather than validation, throwing targets undefined (undefined). The op is registered surface:use, so it is offered to every CLI and op caller yet cannot succeed against any catalog-installed substrate. This is evidence of garbled LLM implementation: a schema refactor that dropped fields without updating the consuming function, compounded by tests that hand-build verifications carrying the removed fields and thereby bypass schema validation, masking the break as a false pass. Every real task completion, including TASK-008 this session, used update-block-item, never the gated op. Agent-investigated for root cause, shape, and reproducible conditions before filing.

evidence.reference: completeTask reads verification.target + verification.target_type === task
canonical packaged schema declares neither target nor target_type (0 matches)
active substrate schema declares neither field
completeTask tests hand-build verifications with target/target_type, bypassing schema validation: false-pass masking the runtime break
commit c08f93b removed target+target_type in favor of the verification_verifies_item edge; completeTask not migrated

gaps.impact: The gated task-completion path (verification-before-complete) is non-functional framework-wide; task completion silently degrades to ungated update-block-item with no verification gate. The masking tests give false confidence the gate works. Architectural debt: the edge-based verification design (verification_verifies_item) is declared but unconsumed by completeTask.
gaps.proposed_resolution: CLOSED at d0ab83d (TASK-014). completeTask migrated from the removed verification.target/target_type fields to a pure verification_verifies_item edge-gate (parent=verification, child=task) + status===passed; task set completed with no verification field embed (the edge is the linkage); ctx threaded. Test suite rewritten to the edge flow (no field smuggling) + a no-edge-bypass case; the legacy defaults/ field-gate test migrated to a canonical schema. Runtime-demoed on real .context (dogfooded: TASK-014 itself completed via the fixed op) + fresh adversarial audit found no bypass. Backfilled the verification_verifies_item edges for the session completions (TASK-008/009/012/013) that update-block-item had left edgeless.

gaps.title: Read cap enforced per-renderer (structureForRead/{read}) not at the output boundary — the {json} channel bypasses it on both CLI --json and in-pi Pi-tool surfaces
gaps.description: Root cause surfaced by the TASK-012 adversarial re-audit. The 50KB read cap + FGAP-089 fail-closed REFUSAL lives inside structureForRead (used only by OpResult {read}); the {json} channel returns a raw value with no cap. So a {json} op embedding substrate content emits it unbounded on BOTH surfaces: the CLI --json envelope (cli.ts:492, output=r.json) and the in-pi Pi-tool tool-result text (ops-registry.ts:1927, renderOpResultText -> JSON.stringify(r.json) into the agent context — the exact context blowup the cap exists to prevent). Two confirmed unbounded {json} emitters: resolve-item-by-id (full ItemLocation incl. item body) and promote-item (PromotionResult embeds an item via ResolvedRef.loc). The one-at-a-time audit discovery (read-block, then resolve-item-by-id, then promote-item) is the signature of the missing invariant. Same gap-class the parity-check gate already closes for op-coverage/ctx-forwarding.

evidence.reference: --json emits output=r.json uncapped for {json} ops
registerAll Pi-tool text = renderOpResultText -> JSON.stringify(r.json) uncapped into agent context
resolve-item-by-id returns { json: full ItemLocation } — unbounded content emitter
promote-item returns { json: PromotionResult } embedding an item via ResolvedRef.loc
structureForRead applies truncateHead; only {read} ops route through it — the cap is a renderer property, not a boundary invariant

gaps.impact: Any op emitting substrate content via {json} leaks it unbounded on both the CLI --json surface and the in-pi agent context, defeating the FGAP-089 fail-closed read cap. Per-op patching is whack-a-mole; the next {json} content-emitter (existing or future) re-opens it.
gaps.proposed_resolution: CLOSED at 75f2611 (TASK-013), per DEC-0009. The read cap is now enforced at the output boundary for every channel: a runtime boundary cap (boundedJsonOutput + cap-applying renderOpResultText) fail-closes any over-cap {json}/prose on all three emit sites (CLI --json, CLI text, in-pi Pi-tool); resolve-item-by-id + promote-item route through {read} for graceful degradation; a parity-check gate (checkJsonContentCap) FAILS an op directly returning {json} of a content-reading call, with the runtime cap as the backstop for helper-chain misses. Adversarial re-audit confirmed the recurrence ended — no read/query op ships unbounded data on either surface. Pre-existing truncateHead pretty-vs-compact/line-cap over-refusal tracked as FGAP-016 (non-leak).

gaps.title: Read cap measures the pretty/line-counted form while --json emits compact — conservative over-refusal + byte-vs-line message imprecision (pre-existing, cross-channel)
gaps.description: Surfaced by the FGAP-015 re-audit (NOT introduced by it). truncateHead measures the pretty JSON.stringify(x, null, 2) byte size AND a 2000-line dimension; the CLI --json envelope emits the COMPACT JSON.stringify(x). So a value whose compact emitted form is well under 50KB but whose pretty form exceeds 50KB, or which exceeds 2000 lines, fails closed under --json even though the emitted compact payload would fit. This is conservative — it over-refuses, never under-protects (cannot leak) — and is inherited truncateHead behavior the {read} channel already exhibits; FGAP-015 applied the same primitive to the {json} boundary cap consistently. Secondary: the REFUSAL message reads 'N bytes, over the 50KB cap' even when the actual trigger is the 2000-line dimension, so the stated reason can be cosmetically inaccurate.

evidence.reference: truncateHead measures pretty-serialized bytes + DEFAULT_MAX_LINES=2000; the cap dimension that fires is not surfaced in the refusal text
--json envelope emits compact JSON.stringify({ok,op,output}); boundedJsonOutput measures the pretty form -> measurement form != emission form
structureForRead/renderReadText measure the pretty content the same way — the {read} channel has the same pre-existing over-refusal

gaps.impact: A legitimate read whose compact --json payload would fit under the cap can be refused (functional over-restriction for machine --json consumers); the refusal message can misstate bytes-vs-lines as the trigger. Safe (never leaks), low severity, but a real ergonomic imprecision spanning the cap primitive and both output channels.
gaps.proposed_resolution: Decide the cap's measurement basis per surface: for --json, measure the EMITTED compact form (so the cap reflects what actually ships); for text/Pi-tool, the pretty form is what the agent reads, so measuring pretty is right there. Surface the firing dimension (bytes vs lines) in the REFUSAL message. Its own canonical-pipeline arc; distinct from FGAP-015's channel-bypass.

gaps.title: context-current-state hardcodes framework-gaps/tasks/phase — inert on custom-vocabulary substrates

No config registry declares which kinds are focus-eligible / next-ranked / blocked-checked, which field gives order/ranking, or which relation_type is blocked-by.

blocked-by hardcoded to relation_type task_depends_on_task
hardcoded priorityRank P0/P1/P2/P3; ranking field hardcoded to priority

gaps.title: context-status lifecycle metrics special-case stock kinds (phase/requirements/verification); custom kinds get only generic summaries
gaps.description: contextState() (context-sdk.ts ~429-667), behind the context-status op, produces a GENERIC per-kind block summary for every block (status aggregation works for custom kinds), but special-cases 5 stock lifecycle blocks with hardcoded fields/status values: phase (status completed), requirements (byStatus + byPriority on field priority), tasks (byStatus), domain (entries total), verification (status passed/failed split). So a custom-vocabulary substrate gets only the generic status summary, not the declared lifecycle metrics the stock kinds enjoy.

generic per-array status-summary path (works for any kind) — the default custom kinds fall back to
context-status op delegates to contextState(cwd)

gaps.title: Block-mutation ops require `arrayKey` though it is derivable from config (CLASS: 7 ops)
gaps.description: Seven block-mutation OpDefinitions in pi-context's op-registry declare `arrayKey` as a required parameter (bare `Type.String`), forcing every caller to restate a value the substrate already declares as `config.block_kinds[].array_key`. pi-context-cli faithfully reflects the op schema's `required` set, so the redundant required flag surfaces at the CLI (e.g. `append-block-item --block research` errors `missing required: --arrayKey` despite `research`'s array_key being knowable from config). The deficiency is in the OP SCHEMAS, one layer below the CLI — the CLI reflection is correct. CLASS, not single-op: append-block-item, update-block-item, upsert-block-item, remove-block-item (top-level array_key), plus the parent `arrayKey` of the 3 nested ops append-block-nested-item / update-block-nested-item / remove-block-nested-item. write-block + read-block are clean (no arrayKey param). For the nested ops, only the parent `arrayKey` is redundant; their `nestedKey` is legitimately caller-supplied (a property of the parent-array item schema, NOT derivable from block_kinds).

evidence.reference: Origin: surfaced by USING the CLI to file research item R-0002 — append-block-item demanded --arrayKey research though array_key is config-declared. Repro (each errors `missing required: --arrayKey`): append-block-item --block research --item @x.json; update-block-item --block issues --match .. --updates ..; upsert-block-item --block decisions --item @x.json; remove-block-item --block .. --match ..
append-block-item parameters declare arrayKey as bare Type.String (required), not Type.Optional — the root-cause shape
append-block-item run() passes params.arrayKey straight to appendToBlock(cwd, block, arrayKey, item, ctx) without consulting config
loadConfig is imported into ops-registry but never called by these ops — the derivation primitive is present and unused

CLI computes required flags solely from schema.required; no per-command hardcoding (no 'append-block-item' literal in cli.ts) — confirms the CLI faithfully reflects the op, so the gap is the op schema
update-block-item: same redundant-required arrayKey shape (params + run pass-through)
upsert-block-item: same redundant-required arrayKey shape
remove-block-item: same redundant-required arrayKey shape
append-block-nested-item: parent arrayKey redundant-required; nestedKey is legitimately required (not config-derivable)
update-block-nested-item: same nested shape (parent arrayKey redundant, nestedKey legitimate)
remove-block-nested-item: same nested shape
loadConfig(cwd):ConfigBlock|null + BlockKindDecl.array_key is the derivation source for the fix
the orchestrator script ALREADY derives array_key from the block (loadBlockSchema/resolveBlockItemSchema) — the op layer is the laggard; the dual surface is therefore asymmetric

gaps.impact: Every block-mutation call (CLI op, orchestrator-script consumer, and in-pi tool dispatch) must restate a config-declared value — redundant friction on the highest-traffic write path, multiplied across 7 ops. Because the orchestrator script already derives array_key while the ops do not, the dual surface is asymmetric — a parity gap the parity-check gate does not currently catch (it tracks optional-param flag divergence, not required-params-that-should-be-derived). Recurring papercut for any agent or human driving the CLI directly.
gaps.proposed_resolution: In each of the 7 affected op run() bodies, derive the top-level array_key from params.block via loadConfig(cwd) -> config.block_kinds.find(bk => bk.canonical_id === block).array_key, and demote the arrayKey parameter from required Type.String to Type.Optional(Type.String(...)) serving as an explicit override (override honored when present; derived when omitted) — backward-compatible for callers still passing --arrayKey. Nested ops keep nestedKey required (not config-derivable); only their parent arrayKey is demoted/derived. Re-confirm via the canonical pipeline (plan -> coding subagent -> adversarial audit). Add/extend a parity-check assertion so required-but-config-derivable params are caught as a class, closing the op<->orchestrator-script asymmetry and preventing regression. Class closure requires exhaustive re-enumeration: grep all OpDefinitions for a required arrayKey to zero after the fix, not just the seed op.

gaps.title: read-schema --path at an object node projects to the `required` name-list, not the node subtree
gaps.description: read-schema --path addressed at a JSON-Schema OBJECT node (e.g. properties.gaps.items) returns a flat list of field NAMES (the node's `required` array) instead of the node's full schema subtree (properties/types/required/additionalProperties). Leaf nodes return correctly (properties.gaps.items.properties.status -> full {type,enum}); the whole-schema read (no --path) returns the full schema. Root cause: the read-schema op routes the addressed node through structureForRead, whose discoverArrayKey heuristic treats any object with a single array-valued property as a paginated collection and pages it — a schema object node carrying a `required` array trips it, so the array is returned in place of the object. The pi-tool op and the reflecting pi-context-cli both project; the orchestrator script scripts/orchestrator/read-schema.ts avoids it by stringifying the addressed value directly — a dual-surface asymmetry (the two surfaces disagree on the same query). Repro: `read-schema --schemaName framework-gaps --path properties.gaps.items` -> ['id','title',...,'created_at'] (the 10-entry required list); `--path properties.gaps.items.properties.status` -> full {type,enum}; no --path -> full schema.

evidence.reference: Origin: surfaced by USING the CLI while filing FGAP-019 — read-schema --schemaName framework-gaps --path properties.gaps.items returned the 10-entry required name-list, hiding that `evidence` is an array; the hidden type caused a downstream `Validation failed ... /gaps/18/evidence: must be array` failure at write time and drove wasteful field-by-field probing.
discoverArrayKey treats an object with a single array-valued property as a paginated collection; a JSON-Schema object node carrying a `required` array trips this heuristic.
resolveCollection resolves the addressed node to that array.
structureForRead returns data = the `required` name-list instead of the full object subtree — the projection itself.
read-schema op passes the --path-addressed node through structureForRead, so an object node with `required` projects; the reflecting pi-context-cli inherits it.
the orchestrator-script counterpart uses JSON.stringify(addr.value) directly and returns the full subtree — dual-surface asymmetry: the script is correct, the op/CLI projects.
no test covers schema-node path addressing / the projection, and no CLI-vs-op output-parity test exists — the regression surface a fix must add.

gaps.impact: Addressing an object node to inspect its shape returns only field names, hiding types/required/enum — the caller believes they read the schema when they did not. This session it directly caused a downstream filing failure (evidence-must-be-array discovered only at write time) and drove wasteful per-field probing. Any agent using read-schema --path to navigate into an object node is misled. The dual-surface asymmetry (op/CLI project; orchestrator script does not) means the two surfaces disagree on the same query.
gaps.proposed_resolution: Carve schema/object nodes out of the collection-paging path: when read-schema addresses a node (or structureForRead is handed an object that is not a genuine paginated collection) return the full subtree — e.g. a whole:true path through structureForRead at ops-registry.ts:1166, or guard discoverArrayKey/resolveCollection (read-element.ts:39-48/202-227) so an object carrying both `properties` and `required` is not paged on `required`. Align the op output with the orchestrator script (read-schema.ts:181) so the dual surface agrees. Add read-element tests for schema-node path addressing + a CLI-vs-op output-parity assertion. Re-confirm via the canonical pipeline (plan -> coding subagent -> adversarial audit). Class check: ensure no OTHER read op routing through structureForRead projects object nodes carrying a single array property.

gaps.title: CLI lacks a human-render mode (--format / terse / --raw) the script twins provide

representative op-twin scripts provide --format table|chain|summary|csv + renderTerse default + --raw; the CLI has no equivalent

formatAjvError translates AJV instancePath/schemaPath into field-named guidance

upsert-block-item op declares dryRun; append-block-item op does not — the asymmetry

gaps.title: In-pi read-schema op returns the projected name-list, not the full subtree (needs whole:true at ops-registry.ts:1166)
gaps.description: Distinct from FGAP-020's CLI-surface correction: the IN-PI read-schema op itself returns the paged `required` name-list for an addressed object node, because it wraps addr.value in structureForRead (ops-registry.ts:1166) which pages on the node's incidental array (read-element.ts:266-285). Correcting BOTH surfaces requires passing whole:true at ops-registry.ts:1166 — a deliberate change to in-pi op behavior, which the parity survey's frozen-ops constraint explicitly carved out and the user has now authorized as a tracked gap.

evidence.reference: read-schema op wraps the addressed node in structureForRead WITHOUT whole:true — the in-pi op projection point; whole:true here forces full-subtree serialization while retaining the 50KB cap
whole:true forces whole-object serialization and skips collection discovery
the orchestrator script already returns the full subtree (JSON.stringify(addr.value)) — the behavior the in-pi op should match
the GAP-2 residual / REQUIRES-OP-CHANGE branch in §D/§E of the parity survey

gaps.impact: In-pi agents (not only the CLI) reading an object schema node via read-schema --path receive the field-name list, not the schema subtree — the same defect class as FGAP-020 but on the in-pi surface, which the CLI-side fix does not reach.

gaps.title: Installable schema descriptions hardcode this project (package names / Muni / in-pi / .project/) — non-portable
gaps.description: The packaged installable vocabulary that /context install copies (packages/pi-context/samples/schemas/*.schema.json) leaks THIS monorepo into description text: 8 lines across 5 of 16 schemas hardcode monorepo package names (pi-workflows, pi-project, pi-behavior-monitors, pi-jit-agents), the 'in-pi' runtime assumption, the '.project/' directory, and 'Muni' as the canonical layer model. An installed or exported model therefore carries this repo's identity into other projects, presuming the installer IS this monorepo — directly undercutting the portability arc (R-0002). conception.json + the other 11 schemas are clean. Separately, layer-plans + work-orders are whole-purpose project-specific (their entire subject is this repo's storage-restructuring / JIT-agent machinery), so for them the question is keep-or-drop from a generic catalog, not just description-stripping.

evidence.reference: properties.location.description: 'File path + line number, e.g. packages/pi-workflows/src/dag.ts:219'
properties.package.description: 'Which monorepo package this issue touches (e.g. pi-workflows, pi-project, pi-behavior-monitors)'
description: '...drives the in-pi bounded code-change loop...' — whole description is this repo's JIT-agent/orchestrator machinery
properties.context_blocks.description: '...per the existing ContextBlockRef shape in packages/pi-jit-agents/src/types.ts'
description: 'Capability gaps in the pi-project-workflows framework — ...'
description: 'restructuring the .project/ directory ... conceptual source (e.g. Muni five-layer)' — whole-purpose project-specific
properties.model.description: "Conceptual source — e.g. 'Muni five-layer'"
properties.layer.description: 'Which Muni layer the research informs. L1 identity/domain; L2 specification; L3 work; L4 execution; L5 memory.'

gaps.impact: Any /context install (or a future export) carries this repo's package names + Muni + .project/ + in-pi into the installing project's schema descriptions — the model presents itself as project-agnostic but is not. Repeats across every consuming project; the leak is the catalog's, so it propagates by design.
gaps.proposed_resolution: Genericize the 8 leaking description substrings in samples/schemas/ per a from->to strip-list (package names -> placeholder/'your agent layer'; 'in-pi' -> dropped; '.project/' -> 'substrate storage'; project layer-model proper noun -> 'layered architecture model'/'architecture layer'); preserve the model's intrinsic vocabulary (relations.json, L1-L5 mapping, substrate); produce a portable disinfect markdown; adversarial re-grep to zero residual named-term leaks. RESOLVED at b1cdccc: the 8 substrings genericized across issues/work-orders/framework-gaps/layer-plans/research; zero residual named-term leaks (grep); read-samples-catalog parses all 16 kinds; check + test green; portable strip-list at analysis/2026-06-05-disinfect-schema-descriptions.md. An adversarial re-audit additionally flagged model-intrinsic vocabulary (relations.json / L1-L5 / substrate / feature-story-task) as 'leakage' — dismissed as scope over-reach (that vocabulary describes what the shipped model IS, not which project authored it). Follow-ons NOT part of this closure: .context/schemas/ installed copies carry the same leaks (re-sync via /context install --update); .context/schemas/layer-plans.schema.json is version-skewed (1.0.0, missing layers[]/migration_phases[]).

gaps.title: /context install --update overwrites block DATA with empty starters (substrate-wiping); stale installed schemas have no safe re-sync; undocumented
gaps.description: installContext (index.ts, behind /context install) copies catalog assets with fs.copyFileSync and, under --update (overwrite=true), overwrites BOTH installed_schemas[] AND installed_blocks[]. The block starters in samples/blocks/*.json are EMPTY ({"<array_key>": []}), so --update copies an empty file over the LIVE block, deleting every filed item. Verified: 'tasks' is in installed_blocks; .context/tasks.json holds 22 items; --update would zero it; all 16 installed blocks (decisions, framework-gaps, research, ...) are equally exposed. No migration, no version check, no warning, no backup. Root consequence: under the never-clobber (skip-if-exists) default, installed SCHEMAS silently drift stale from the evolving catalog (confirmed: .context/schemas/layer-plans.schema.json is 1.0.0 vs catalog 1.0.1, missing layers[]/migration_phases[]) — and the ONLY mechanism to re-sync them, --update, ALSO destroys all block data. So there is no safe path to refresh a stale installed schema.

evidence.reference: installContext blocks loop: if destExists && overwrite -> fs.copyFileSync(sourceFile, destFile) where destFile is <substrate>/<name>.json (the LIVE block) and sourceFile is the empty samples/blocks starter — blind full-file overwrite, no item preservation
starter is empty: {"tasks": []} — what --update copies over the live tasks.json
live block holds 22 items (TASK-001..022) that --update would silently delete; verified via grep item count
schemas loop: --update overwrites the installed schema via copyFileSync with NO migration / version-skew check, so a schema re-sync can desync from existing items' schema_version
docs say 'Default behavior is skip-if-exists (preserves user edits); pass --update to overwrite' — frames --update as safe edit re-sync, SILENT on block-data loss

gaps.impact: A user who runs /context install --update to refresh schemas (the documented re-sync action) silently destroys every filed item in every block — total substrate data loss. Conversely, stale installed schemas have NO safe re-sync at all, since the only re-sync mechanism is the destructive one. The never-clobber default also means installed copies drift from the catalog indefinitely with no surfaced signal. High-severity footgun on a routine-sounding flag.
gaps.proposed_resolution: Separate schema/asset re-sync from block-data: never overwrite a POPULATED block (write a starter only when the block is absent); route installed-schema evolution through the EXISTING migration registry (migrations.json / validateBlockWithMigration) rather than blind copyFileSync, so items migrate forward instead of being orphaned; surface a dry-run / diff preview (what would change) before any overwrite; detect + report installed-vs-catalog version skew so staleness is visible; document the never-clobber default + the destructive scope. The specific mechanism to be chosen from prior-art in similarly-situated functionality (template-update reconcilers like copier/cruft 3-way merge; DB schema-migration frameworks Flyway/Alembic/Prisma/Rails; declarative reconcilers with plan/apply like kubectl apply / Terraform) — minimal, correct, best-of-breed. Run through the canonical pipeline.
gaps.closed_by: human/davidryan@gmail.com — closed by fa8a0cc (skipIdentityStamp excision, DEC-0012) + c6434db (re-sync promoted to live .context); verified by VER-014. Core safe re-sync resolved: install --update routes installed-schema evolution through the migration registry, never blind-overwrites a populated block (S1), records a baseline (S2), previews drift (S3 check-status), and forward-migrates-or-refuses (S4).
