gaps.title: check-status drift report is command-only — no context-check-status op, unreachable from the reflecting CLI
gaps.description: The pure-read schema-drift report (checkStatus in packages/pi-context/src/index.ts, rendered by renderCheckStatus) is surfaced ONLY as the /context check-status command handler (CONTEXT_SUBCOMMANDS in index.ts). There is no OpDefinition for it in the op-registry, and the reflecting pi-context-cli only surfaces surface:"use" ops — so check-status is unreachable from the CLI (a human or agent driving node packages/pi-context-cli/dist/bin.js cannot get the drift report). This is at odds with the CLI-parity direction (R-0003) and inconsistent with how /context status pairs with the context-status op. checkStatus is pure-read (returns CheckStatusReport, writes nothing), so it is a clean op candidate with no writer-classification or cap concerns.

evidence.reference: checkStatus(cwd): CheckStatusReport (pure-read) + the 'check-status' entry in CONTEXT_SUBCOMMANDS — command handler only
no context-check-status OpDefinition; the op-registry does not expose the drift report
useOps = ops.filter(o => o.surface === 'use') — the CLI reflects ops only, so a command-only surface is invisible to it

gaps.impact: The drift report is in-pi-only; the reflecting pi-context-cli (and orchestrator-script consumers) cannot invoke check-status, undercutting the CLI-parity direction. Same family as the FGAP-021..026 CLI-parity gaps.
gaps.proposed_resolution: Add a context-check-status OpDefinition (surface:"use", empty parameters, run(cwd) -> { json: checkStatus(cwd) } — pure-read, boundedJsonOutput-capped) so the reflecting pi-context-cli surfaces it automatically. OP-ONLY — NO orchestrator-script twin (per DEC-0011: new ops are op-only; the reflecting CLI is the shell surface; hand-writing a script twin would be a born-obsolete artifact). Pure-read, so no writer-classification/allowlist needed. Run through the canonical pipeline.

gaps.title: Target state: invoke pi-context-cli as the first-class `pi-context` command, not `node .../dist/bin.js`
gaps.description: packages/pi-context-cli/package.json already declares `bin: { "pi-context": "./dist/bin.js" }` and dist/bin.js carries a `#!/usr/bin/env node` shebang — so installing or linking the package puts a first-class `pi-context` command on PATH. But the dev workflow, the docs (CLAUDE.md, READMEs), the dogfooding invocations, and orchestration all call `node packages/pi-context-cli/dist/bin.js <op>` — the developer-mode path-to-bin wart, not a real CLI. TARGET STATE: pi-context is a first-rate command invoked as `pi-context <op> --...` everywhere; no `node /path/to/bin` ceremony.

evidence.reference: bin: { pi-context: ./dist/bin.js } — the first-class command is already declared
#!/usr/bin/env node shebang present — directly executable once on PATH
the canonical filing patterns + dogfooding all show `node packages/pi-context-cli/dist/bin.js <op>` rather than `pi-context <op>`

gaps.impact: Every CLI invocation (docs, dogfooding, scripts, other-agent shell access) carries the path-to-bin wart; the CLI does not present as a first-rate installed command, which is friction, non-portable, and a poorer dogfooding/UX signal than a real `pi-context <op>` command.
gaps.proposed_resolution: Make `pi-context` reachable in the dev monorepo (npm link, or the npm-workspaces node_modules/.bin symlink via `npx pi-context` / .bin on PATH) and switch the docs (CLAUDE.md + READMEs + skill-narrative) and dogfooding/orchestration invocations to `pi-context <op>`; the published package already gives end users the global command. Confirm the linked/installed entrypoint resolves and runs identically to the in-repo bin. Run through the canonical pipeline.
gaps.closed_by: TASK-028 (VER-017) made pi-context globally installable (chmod +x dist/bin.js + the published bin) and the project docs + dogfooding switched to the global `pi-context <op>` command, retiring `node …/dist/bin.js`; `which pi-context` resolves the published bin. The load-bearing asks (first-class command on PATH + doc/dogfooding switch) are met.

gaps.title: pi-context-cli id-flag naming diverges across id-taking ops (--id vs --itemId vs --taskId vs --parentId vs --unitId), with no normalized convention
gaps.description: The reflecting pi-context-cli derives each op's CLI flag names verbatim from that op's typebox parameters-schema property keys (packages/pi-context-cli/src/cli.ts parseOpArgs reads props[field] off objectSchema(op).properties; an unknown key throws 'unknown flag'). Because the op-registry authored those property names ad hoc per op, the single-item-id argument is spelled differently across ops with no shared convention: read-block-item and resolve-item-by-id use --id; find-references and walk-ancestors use --itemId; context-walk-descendants uses --parentId; complete-task uses --taskId; gather-execution-context uses --unitId; promote-item uses --source. update-block-item / remove-block-item / nested-item ops select the id inside a --match JSON predicate; relation ops use --parent / --child. Driving the CLI, the natural guess --id fails on find-references with 'unknown flag: --id' (exit 2), forcing a re-read of --help to discover --itemId. The divergence is purely a property-name authoring inconsistency in the op-registry surfaced 1:1 by the flag-derivation, not a behavioral defect: each op works once the right flag is supplied. Impact is dogfooding friction (wrong-flag guesses, failed invocations, help round-trips) rather than data correctness.

evidence.reference: resolve-item-by-id parameters declare 'id: Type.String' — id-flag spelled --id
read-block-item parameters declare 'id: Type.String' — id-flag spelled --id
walk-ancestors parameters declare 'itemId: Type.String' — same single-item-id concept spelled --itemId, diverging from --id
find-references parameters declare 'itemId: Type.String' — id-flag spelled --itemId; calling with --id throws 'unknown flag: --id' (exit 2), the trigger of this gap
context-walk-descendants parameters declare 'parentId: Type.String' — id-flag spelled --parentId
complete-task parameters declare 'taskId: Type.String' — id-flag spelled --taskId
gather-execution-context parameters declare 'unitId: Type.String' — id-flag spelled --unitId

parseOpArgs derives flag names 1:1 from objectSchema(op).properties keys (props[field]); a token whose stripped name is absent from properties throws UsageError('unknown flag: ' + tok). The CLI has no id-flag aliasing/normalization layer, so each op's schema property-name spelling is the only accepted flag for that op's item-id argument.

gaps.impact: Usability/dogfooding footgun, not a correctness defect. Each id-taking op works once the correct flag is supplied; the cost is wrong-flag guesses and failed invocations (exit 2) when driving the CLI directly. The most common single-item-id spelling is --id (read-block-item, resolve-item-by-id), so --id is the natural guess and the ops that diverge from it (find-references, walk-ancestors as --itemId; context-walk-descendants as --parentId) reliably mis-fire. Because the CLI auto-reflects op-registry property names, every future id-taking op inherits whatever spelling its author chose, so the divergence grows unless a convention is established.
gaps.proposed_resolution: Establish a single canonical convention for the single-item-id argument across id-taking ops; the mechanism is to be chosen canonically. Options: (a) normalize the op-registry property names so every single-item-id parameter is the same key (rename --itemId/--parentId/--unitId/--taskId to a shared spelling at the schema source, accepting that this changes the op input-schema property name as seen by both the Pi tool surface and the CLI); (b) add an id-flag alias layer in pi-context-cli parseOpArgs (packages/pi-context-cli/src/cli.ts) that accepts a canonical --id alongside each op's native id-property flag, leaving op-registry schemas untouched (CLI-only ergonomic, no Pi-tool surface change); or (c) introduce a per-op-registry-entry declarative alias map the CLI consumes. Whichever is chosen, also assess relation/match-predicate ops (--parent/--child, --match) for whether a consistent id-selection idiom is warranted. Add a parity/lint check that flags new id-taking ops whose item-id property deviates from the convention.

gaps.title: pre-identity substrate schema re-sync is unsupported — install --update refuses (blocks) a populated-block version bump on a substrate without an established substrate_id
gaps.description: DEC-0012 reverted the skipIdentityStamp escape hatch, restoring mandatory identity-stamping on every write. As a result, resyncSchema's migrate write (writeBlockForDir) now requires an established substrate_id: on a substrate that has none, stampWholeBlockIdentity's substrateIdForDir throws inside resyncSchema's try and is caught as the 'blocked' refuse path — the schema and block are left byte-unchanged and the version bump is refused rather than migrated. So /context install --update cannot forward-migrate a populated block on a pre-identity substrate. This is the deferred consequence DEC-0012 names ('Any genuine requirement for resync to support a pre-identity substrate will be designed via canonical process — a declared gap, a dedicated DEC, and a scoped plan'); this is that declared gap.

evidence.reference: resyncSchema's migrate path calls writeBlockForDir(destRoot, name, migrated); on a pre-identity substrate the stamping throw is caught by the surrounding try/catch and the outcome becomes 'blocked' (verified by runtime demo: pre-identity -> blocked, schema+block byte-unchanged, no uncaught throw)
substrateIdForDir throws when config.json carries no valid substrate_id (expected ^sub-[0-9a-f]{16}$) — the loud guard that now fires on the resync write path for a pre-identity substrate

two S4 migrate-path tests ('version bump WITH a shipped identity migration ... -> migrated' and 'after a migrate, check-status reports the schema in-sync') are it.skip'd pending this work — both run on a pre-identity makeProject substrate

gaps.impact: A pre-identity substrate (no substrate_id) cannot use /context install --update to forward-migrate a populated block — the re-sync silently refuses (blocked) rather than migrating. No data loss (schema + block byte-unchanged), but the operation is unavailable for pre-identity substrates and the two skipped tests leave that path without green coverage. Identity-established substrates (the normal case; live .context carries a substrate_id) are unaffected.
gaps.proposed_resolution: Design via canonical process (a dedicated DEC + scoped plan) how schema re-sync should behave for a pre-identity substrate — e.g. whether resync should establish/require identity first, persist items under an explicit pre-identity contract, or remain refused-by-design. Once decided, re-green (or replace per the chosen contract) the two skipped tests.

gaps.title: Phase schema carries no success_criteria field
gaps.description: Composite levels (phase, milestone) carry no success_criteria. A phase is done when its tasks are done. The phase schema's optional success_criteria field is removed.

evidence.reference: declares an optional success_criteria field; the model forbids composite-level criteria, so it is removed

gaps.impact: Phase schema declares an optional success_criteria field the model forbids; composite-level criteria duplicate the child rollup.
gaps.proposed_resolution: Remove success_criteria from the phase schema; phase completeness derives from its tasks.

gaps.title: Task success_criteria are objects, replacing acceptance_criteria
gaps.description: The task field acceptance_criteria is replaced by success_criteria. Each entry is an object { statement, status: pass|fail, verified_by, verified_at, evidence }. statement is binary and outcome-based. A task is done when every entry is pass.

evidence.reference: task items declare acceptance_criteria, not success_criteria

gaps.impact: Task schema declares acceptance_criteria as a string array; strings carry no status or proof, so done-ness and verification are not machine-checkable from the criterion.
gaps.proposed_resolution: Rename acceptance_criteria to success_criteria and change each item from string to the criterion object.

gaps.title: Phase and task criteria must be binary outcome-based or invalid
gaps.description: Both levels' criteria must be binary outcome-based or invalid — phase success_criteria and task success_criteria must each be binary and outcome-based, or they are invalid.

evidence.reference: no constraint that phase success_criteria are binary and outcome-based
no constraint that task criteria are binary and outcome-based

gaps.impact: Criteria at both levels can be non-binary or non-outcome-based.
gaps.proposed_resolution: Constrain phase and task success_criteria so each criterion must be binary and outcome-based, or it is invalid.

gaps.title: No milestones block — add a milestone block kind (samples catalog + active .context)
gaps.description: There is no milestone block kind, yet phases need a checkpoint to roll up into. Add a milestone block. Fields: id (MILE-NNN), name, status (planned | reached, no missed), optional release to group gaps/issues/tasks/phases into a release (e.g. 0.30.1). No intent field. No evidence_block/evidence_query. reached is derived: a milestone is reached when its placed phases are done (placement relation, FGAP-039); stories attach to it via the wildcard relation (FGAP-041). Lands in both the packaged samples catalog (via the pi-context update CLI) and the active .context substrate.

evidence.reference: block_kinds registry lists 17 kinds (decisions, framework-gaps, tasks, verification, issues, features, research, rationale, spec-reviews, layer-plans, requirements, conventions, context-contracts, phase, story, work-orders, session-notes) — none is 'milestone'
phase description references story -> milestone/phase/project-goal edges, but no milestone block kind exists
story description names milestone as a cross-altitude edge target; no milestone block backs it

gaps.impact: Phases and stories reference milestones as edge targets with nothing to point at, and there is no block to roll gaps/issues/tasks/phases into a release — milestones cannot be represented in the substrate.
gaps.proposed_resolution: Add a milestone block kind with id (MILE-NNN), name, status (planned|reached), optional release; no intent; reached derived from placed phases (FGAP-039); organized by stories via FGAP-041. Land it in both the samples catalog and the active .context.

gaps.title: Verification block retired; proof lives on the task criterion
gaps.description: The verification block kind and the verification_verifies_item relation are removed. A task success_criterion carries its own proof inline (verified_by, verified_at, evidence). Existing verification items fold into the criteria they attest, or are dropped.

evidence.reference: block_kinds registry includes 'verification'; relation_types registry includes 'verification_verifies_item'
holds verification items that duplicate the pass/proof a criterion now carries

gaps.impact: Two constructs assert the same done-ness: the criterion's pass and a separate verification item.
gaps.proposed_resolution: Delete the verification block kind and the verification_verifies_item relation; migrate each verification onto the task criterion it attests.

gaps.title: Milestone groups phases via a placement relation
gaps.description: A phase is placed in a milestone by a relation mirroring task_positioned_in_phase. A milestone is reached when its placed phases are done.

evidence.reference: relation_types has task_positioned_in_phase (tasks->phase) but no phase->milestone placement; milestone block absent (FGAP-037)

gaps.impact: Milestone rollup has no edge to read; phases cannot be grouped under a milestone.
gaps.proposed_resolution: Register a phase-in-milestone placement relation_type; milestone done-ness reads up it.

gaps.title: Story schema: id, description, status (draft|set), type (open string)
gaps.description: A story holds id, a one-sentence description, status draft|set, and a type. type is a free-form string (not an enum) so values such as user or llm-user are admitted without a schema change. title and acceptance_criteria are not present; stories carry no criteria.

evidence.reference: declares title, description, acceptance_criteria, and status enum proposed/ready/in-progress/in-review/complete/blocked

gaps.impact: Story schema carries criteria and a multi-state lifecycle; the model treats a story as a theme with no done-ness of its own.
gaps.proposed_resolution: Set story fields to id + description + status(draft|set) + type, where type is a free-form string (not an enum) admitting values like user or llm-user; drop title, acceptance_criteria, and the surplus status values proposed/ready/in-progress/in-review/complete/blocked.

gaps.title: Stories attach to any item via one wildcard relation
gaps.description: One relation story_includes_item (source story, target any kind) links a story to any task, phase, or milestone. A story's scope is its outbound story_includes_item edges.

evidence.reference: relation_types has story_contains_task (story->tasks) only; no story->any relation

gaps.impact: Stories can only link to tasks; the model needs a story to span any level.
gaps.proposed_resolution: Register story_includes_item with source_kinds=[story], target_kinds=[*].

gaps.title: Roadmap is a view over ordered milestones; ordering is a relation
gaps.description: Milestone order is a milestone-to-milestone ordering relation. The roadmap is a derived view over milestones in that order. No roadmap block exists.

evidence.reference: hand-authored static milestones (const MILESTONES = [...]), not derived; no milestone ordering relation; no roadmap block or lens

gaps.impact: The roadmap has no live source and milestone sequence is unrepresented.
gaps.proposed_resolution: Register relation_type milestone_precedes_milestone (category ordering, source milestone, target milestone), modeled on the existing decision_supersedes_decision. Roadmap order is the precedes-chain: start at the milestone with no predecessor and follow precedes to the end. Render the roadmap as a view over that chain; add no block.

gaps.title: Schema descriptions carry no per-block rhetorical criteria; block writes are unvalidated against them
gaps.description: Each block schema's description states the block's purpose but omits the block's specific rhetorical demands/criteria for its entries (terseness, signal-density, self-containment, appropriateness to block type and downstream use, no narration or git/live-source restatement). The criteria must be embedded per-block and machine-checkable so a Claude Code hook can validate a block-item write against them at write time.

evidence.reference: the 'Rhetorical situation for every block write' rule lives only as operating-doc prose — not encoded per-schema, not enforced on write
item descriptions state field purpose but carry no per-block rhetorical criteria; nothing validates an entry against them on write

gaps.impact: Per-block authoring criteria are unenforced; off-contract entries (provenance narration, git restatement, non-terse prose) pass validation — degrading the verbatim context handed downstream to subagents (garbage in/out).
gaps.proposed_resolution: Validate block-item writes against the rhetorical-register convention (the single canon source) at write time: a PreToolUse hook intercepts append-block-item / update-block-item, composes the rhetorical-register convention, and checks the entry against it — LLM-judge where the register rules are not mechanically checkable — blocking or flagging a non-conforming write and surfacing the register rules so the filer writes to them. The rhetorical-register convention is the canon; do not duplicate criteria per-schema. Per-block specificity, where needed beyond the general register, is added to the relevant block schema's description and composed alongside the convention.

gaps.title: No field-granular schema-edit op; changing one schema property requires whole-document write-schema replace
gaps.description: Schema evolution exposes only whole-document write-schema (operation create|replace); there is no field-granular schema edit. Changing one property (add/rename/retype, bump version) requires supplying and overwriting the entire schema JSON, unlike block items which have update-block-item for field-level updates.

evidence.reference: write-schema operation enum is create|replace, whole-document; no granular per-property operation
update-block-item provides match+updates field-granular item edits — the absent analogue for schema documents

gaps.impact: A one-field schema change carries whole-document blast radius: an unrelated edit in the replaced JSON lands silently, and the error surface grows as the PM-model arc evolves many schemas through repeated full-document replaces.
gaps.proposed_resolution: Add a field-granular schema-edit op mirroring update-block-item's match+updates shape: add/replace/remove a single schema property and bump version in one call, meta-validated, leaving untouched properties byte-stable.

gaps.title: No one-step block-kind rename tool
gaps.description: Block-kind renames require a manual multi-op migration: create a new schema, register a new block_kind, register installed_schemas/installed_blocks entries, write migrated block data, replace relation_type endpoint kinds, optionally rename relation_type ids, remove old config entries, and validate. rename-canonical-id explicitly does not support block_kind, and no harness op performs the filesystem/config/schema/data/relation cascade as one validated transaction.

evidence.reference: rename-canonical-id supports kind item | relation_type | lens | layer, not block_kind
story block_kind rename to user-stories requires coordinated changes across block_kinds, installed_schemas, installed_blocks, schemas, block data, and relation_type endpoint kinds

gaps.impact: Renaming a block kind is high-friction and error-prone inside the pi harness: operators must sequence multiple config/schema/block writes by hand, old physical files can remain orphaned, and partial failure can leave config, relations, schemas, and data out of sync.
gaps.proposed_resolution: Add a first-class block-kind rename operation that performs the full cascade transactionally: create/rename schema and data surfaces, update block_kinds and installed registries, migrate block data, update relation_type source_kinds/target_kinds and hierarchy/lens references, optionally rename relation_type ids by policy, validate the resulting substrate, and report or clean orphaned old files.

gaps.title: install --update clobbers user schema customizations; no customization-preserving merge
gaps.description: install --update overwrites each installed schema with the catalog copy — verbatim on a same-version resync, migrate-or-refuse on a version gap — so a user's edits to a shipped block schema are lost on update. Block item data is preserved (S1) and a user-added custom block kind is untouched (no catalog counterpart), but edits to a catalog-shipped schema have no merge path: update is replace.

evidence.reference: resyncSchema overwrites the installed schema with the catalog version on install --update; same-version resync is a verbatim overwrite, version-gap is migrate-or-refuse — neither reconciles local schema edits

gaps.impact: A non-developer user who tailors a shipped schema must choose: take the update and lose the customization, or run plain install (never-clobber) and forgo the update. Tailoring a catalog schema is fragile against the next --update.
gaps.proposed_resolution: Two candidate routes. (1) Deterministic three-way merge on install --update: reconcile the catalog schema change against the user's local edits (base = the previously-installed catalog version), preserving non-conflicting edits and surfacing conflicts instead of overwriting; or an overlay that re-applies recorded customizations after the catalog refresh. (2) Agent-mediated update in pi: for users tracking the samples, a mechanism by which the pi agent reconciles the user's local schema against the updated samples — applying the catalog change while preserving the user's customizations by judgement rather than a deterministic merge.
gaps.closed_by: VER-023 / TASK-037 — FEAT-006 hybrid complete (refuse T1 -> base-stamp T2 -> 3-way merge T3 -> resolver T4): pi-context update merges a locally-edited schema with the catalog preserving non-conflicting edits, and routes conflicts to an interactive pi-bound mergetool or a conflict report; a customization is never silently clobbered

gaps.title: pi-context has no global command form — the built bin carries no exec bit
gaps.description: packages/pi-context-cli declares bin pi-context -> dist/bin.js with a node shebang, but its build (rm -rf dist && tsc) never sets the exec bit on dist/bin.js, leaving it -rw-r--r--. npm link and npm i -g cannot expose pi-context on PATH from a non-executable entry.

evidence.reference: build is 'rm -rf dist && tsc -p tsconfig.build.json' — no chmod +x dist/bin.js step
built bin is -rw-r--r-- (no exec bit); shebang present

gaps.impact: pi-context is invokable only as `node packages/pi-context-cli/dist/bin.js` from the repo; there is no PATH `pi-context` command for users, and pi-context --pi-bound (FEAT-005) has no global entrypoint.
gaps.proposed_resolution: Add a chmod +x dist/bin.js step to the pi-context-cli build so npm link and npm i -g expose an executable pi-context on PATH; the published artifact carries the bit unconditionally.
gaps.closed_by: human/davidryan@gmail.com — closed by a5055ad (chmod +x dist/bin.js in the pi-context-cli build); verified by VER-017 (dist/bin.js -rwxr-xr-x; npm link exposed pi-context on PATH; ran an op from outside the repo). Published npm i -g smoke deferred (release-gated), not blocking closure of the exec-bit gap.

--pi-bound resolves the meta root via require.resolve('@davidorex/pi-project-workflows/package.json')

gaps.title: install --update overwrites a locally-modified schema without consulting the checkStatus drift classification
gaps.description: The update path overwrites installed schemas (resyncSchema) without first consulting the checkStatus drift detector, which already classifies each installed schema as in-sync / catalog-ahead / locally-modified / both-diverged. A locally-modified (user-edited) schema is clobbered with no detection or warning, even though the classification that would flag it is computable and shipped.

evidence.reference: resyncSchema overwrites the installed schema (verbatim same-version, migrate-or-refuse on bump) without reading the checkStatus locally-modified classification
checkStatus computes the drift classification but the --update write path never calls it before clobbering

gaps.impact: User schema edits are destroyed silently on update; the drift signal needed to prevent it exists but is not consulted in the write path.
gaps.proposed_resolution: The update path consults checkStatus before re-syncing each schema and refuses-or-merges (per FGAP-046) any locally-modified schema instead of overwriting it; delivered within the unified update command (FEAT-006).
gaps.closed_by: FEAT-006 unified update command — VER-020 (TASK-034) delivered the consult-checkStatus routing: updateContext reads the checkStatus drift state per installed schema and routes locally-modified/both-diverged to refuse (T1) / merge (T3, VER-022) / resolve (T4, VER-023) instead of overwriting; the drift classification is consulted before any write

gaps.title: install --update appends catalog migration declarations into the user's migrations.json without surfacing them
gaps.description: On a schema version bump, the update path registers the shipped catalog migration chain into the substrate's migrations.json before forward-validating the block, but the install summary does not report which declarations were added. The user's migrations.json is mutated with no surfaced record of the additions.

evidence.reference: resyncSchema registers each shipped migration declaration into migrations.json on a version bump; the install summary does not enumerate the registered declarations

gaps.impact: A user cannot see, from the update output, which migration declarations were written into their migrations.json; the mutation is silent.
gaps.proposed_resolution: The update output enumerates every migration declaration registered into migrations.json; --dry-run lists them without writing; delivered within the unified update command (FEAT-006).
gaps.closed_by: VER-026 / TASK-039 — pi-context update enumerates the migration declarations it registers into migrations.json under UpdateResult.migrationsRegistered; --dryRun lists the would-register set read-only (computeWouldRegisterMigrations), writing nothing.

gaps.title: install --update rewrites an existing catalog-origin empty block file with the starter (unguarded, currently benign)
gaps.description: Under --update the block branch overwrites a catalog-origin block file that exists on disk but holds no items with the catalog's empty starter, with no short-circuit for the already-present-and-equal case. No data is lost today (empty -> empty), but the rewrite is unguarded and runs on every update.

evidence.reference: the block branch overwrites an existing empty catalog-origin block with the starter under --update rather than skipping an already-equal file

gaps.impact: Benign today (no item data lost). Filed for completeness of the update path's clobber semantics and to make the update idempotent at the file level — an unguarded rewrite of an on-disk file is a latent surface for loss if empty blocks ever carry non-item content.

gaps.title: install --update does not propagate catalog config-registry additions (relation_types/invariants/block_kinds) to an existing substrate
gaps.description: installContext writes config via a verbatim spread ({...config, installed_from}), so new catalog config-registry entries (relation_types, invariants, block_kinds, lenses) are never merged into an existing substrate's config on install --update; adoptConception writes registries only on a skeleton. A catalog model addition (e.g. new governance relation_types/invariants) therefore reaches only fresh substrates; existing substrates never receive it. Distinct from FGAP-046 (schema clobber): this is non-propagation of config-registry additions, not destruction of edits.

evidence.reference: installContext writes config via writeConfig(cwd, {...config, installed_from}) — verbatim spread; no catalog-registry merge
adoptConception writes registries only on a skeleton (isSkeletonConfig guard)

gaps.impact: Any catalog vocabulary/invariant addition is delivered only to fresh installs; existing users must hand-amend-config to receive it — capping the value of every catalog release for the installed base.
gaps.proposed_resolution: The unified update command (FEAT-006) merges catalog config-registry additions into an existing substrate's config (additive, customization-preserving), so new relation_types/invariants/block_kinds reach existing substrates; surface what is added, and --dry-run previews.
gaps.closed_by: VER-025 / TASK-038 — pi-context update additively merges catalog-new config-registry entries (relation_types/invariants/block_kinds/lenses) absent from an existing substrate config, preserving user entries; --dryRun previews. mergeCatalogRegistries (additive, REGISTRY_DESCRIPTORS-driven) wired into updateContext after the baseline-refresh loop.

gaps.title: ready/blocked derivation ignores the *_gated_by_item gating relations; only task_depends_on_task is honored
gaps.description: currentState (context-sdk.ts:682), the sole ready/blocked/next deriver, computes blocked + nextActions ordering only from task_depends_on_task (depParentsOf, context-sdk.ts:727-730). It ignores the gating relations task_gated_by_item / feature_gated_by_item / story_gated_by_item, so a task gated by an incomplete item is reported ready. feature_gated_by_item / story_gated_by_item have no readiness deriver at all (currentState buckets only the tasks block). roadmap-plan.ts orders phases by phase_depends_on (no phase_gated_by_item; out of scope).

evidence.reference: depParentsOf filters only task_depends_on_task; feeds blocked (:742) + topoSort order (:787)
currentState is the sole ready/blocked deriver; buckets only the tasks block — no feature/story readiness derivation exists
context-current-state op description promises ranked unblocked/blocked tasks — wrong while gates are ignored

gaps.impact: Orderings expressed as gates (task_gated_by_item; feature/story_gated_by_item) are invisible to 'what is ready': a gated task surfaces as a next-action, and feature/story gates have no derivation. Cross-arc orderings recorded as gates are documentation-only (carried in a tracker md), not derived.
gaps.proposed_resolution: NOW (task level): extend currentState's depParentsOf (context-sdk.ts:727-730) to also fold in task_gated_by_item parents, treating a task gated by an incomplete/unclosed item as blocked (resolving non-task gate targets to a complete/closed bucket), so blocked + the nextActions topo order honor gates. FORWARD (the proper home, FEAT-004): FEAT-004's config-declared blocked-by relation_type must be a SET including the gating relations (task_/feature_/story_gated_by_item) alongside the *_depends_on relations, and FEAT-004 must add feature/story-level readiness derivation — so gate-awareness is config-declared and covers all kinds, not hardcoded to tasks. Until FEAT-004, the currentState extension closes only the task-level case.

gaps.title: pi-context --help top-level listing is an unscannable flat dump — full LLM tool-descriptions, no grouping, promptSnippet unused, pi-bound undiscoverable
gaps.description: deriveTopHelp (cli.ts:406-422) renders one flat, registration-ordered line per useOps entry (49 ops) under a single 'Commands:' header, sourcing op.description — the full multi-sentence LLM-facing tool description. Its .split("\n")[0] summary attempt is inert: op descriptions are newline-free string concatenations, so the entire paragraph renders (several lines wide for ops like context-bootstrap-state / write-schema-migration). The purpose-built one-line field promptSnippet (present on all 49 OpDefinitions, ops-registry.ts:205) is never read; the name-prefix grouping signal (context-* / read-* / write-* / *-relation(s) / *-block(-nested)-item / roadmap) is unused; there is no sort. pi-bound (a hardcoded process-mode string branch, cli.ts:444, outside the ops registry) is structurally unreachable by the registry-only generator, so a real working command is undiscoverable via --help. Per-op help (deriveHelp, cli.ts:382-403) renders flags + per-field types/descriptions correctly — the defect is the top-level listing only. Reproduce via: pi-context --help (also reached by pi-context with no args, or any unknown command).

evidence.reference: deriveTopHelp: flat loop over useOps (line 409); line 410 sources op.description; split('\n')[0] inert on newline-free descriptions; no grouping/sort/short-summary

OpDefinition carries promptSnippet (one-line summary, on all 49 use-ops) + label (Title Case), both unused by deriveTopHelp; no explicit category/group field — name-prefix is the available grouping signal
pi-bound dispatched as a process-mode branch before resolveOp; not in ops; deriveTopHelp iterates only useOps so pi-bound never appears in --help
per-op help (deriveHelp) is correct (flags + field types/descriptions) — scope of the defect is top-level only

gaps.impact: The reflecting CLI is the dogfooding surface and the new-user entry point; its top-level --help is unscannable (49 paragraph-wide lines), buries the everyday ops (read-block-item / append-block-item / update-block-item / append-relation / context-validate), and hides pi-bound. Both new users and dogfooding hit a wall at the first command run.
gaps.proposed_resolution: Confined to cli.ts deriveTopHelp; data-driven from existing OpDefinition fields (no ops-package change; zero risk to the in-pi LLM tool surface, which projects op.description via registerAll, not deriveTopHelp): (1) source each command line from op.promptSnippet ?? op.description (the existing short summary, not the paragraph); (2) group ops into sections by name-prefix (reads / writes / relations / schema / roadmap / substrate-lifecycle), sorted within section; (3) add a process-modes section listing pi-bound so it is discoverable; optionally flag authGated ops. Do NOT shorten op.description in place — it is the load-bearing LLM tool description.

gaps.title: pi-context CLI has no --version / -v command — a globally-installed CLI cannot report its own version
gaps.description: main() (cli.ts:437-545) has no version branch; `pi-context --version` and `pi-context -v` fall through to resolveOp and are rejected as `unknown command: --version` (exit 2) followed by the help dump. The package ships a `bin` and is globally installed on PATH, yet the command cannot report its own version. `-h`/`--help` is handled (cli.ts:440), but `-v`/`--version` has no equivalent.

evidence.reference: main() has no --version/-v branch; both fall through to resolveOp as an unknown command and exit 2
-h is aliased to help; there is no -v/--version equivalent

gaps.impact: A user verifying an install or filing a bug cannot state which pi-context version they run; --version is a baseline expectation of any installed CLI. A first-run and support deficiency on the global command.
gaps.proposed_resolution: Add a --version/-v branch in main() (beside the -h/--help branch, before resolveOp) that prints the pi-context-cli package version (read from package.json) and returns 0.

gaps.title: pi-context CLI surfaces camelCase op-schema keys as flags verbatim — no kebab-case normalization, so conventional --dry-run / --id fail
gaps.description: parseOpArgs (cli.ts:184-233) derives each op's accepted flag names 1:1 from its typebox parameter property keys; a token whose stripped name is absent from the schema throws `unknown flag` (exit 2). Op-schema keys are camelCase, so the conventional kebab-case flag a user types fails: `pi-context update --dry-run` -> `unknown flag: --dry-run` (only `--dryRun` works), although FEAT-006, FGAP-050/051/060, TASK-038/039 and standard CLI convention all write it `--dry-run`. The CLI has no kebab->camel normalization or alias layer. FGAP-032 is the id-flag-specific instance of this same root (--id vs --itemId/--taskId across ops); THIS gap is the general flag-casing/normalization defect, of which the headline update --dry-run failure is the most visible case.

evidence.reference: parseOpArgs derives flags verbatim from camelCase typebox keys; no kebab->camel normalization; an unknown stripped name throws UsageError (exit 2)
the update op declares parameter key dryRun (camelCase) -> CLI flag --dryRun; the conventional --dry-run is rejected
runtime: `pi-context update --dry-run` -> error: unknown flag: --dry-run, exit 2 (only --dryRun is accepted)

gaps.impact: The headline update feature's safety-preview flag fails when typed in the conventional kebab form documented across the substrate; every camelCase op parameter inherits the same footgun. A first-run and dogfooding UX defect on the highest-visibility surface.
gaps.proposed_resolution: Normalize flag matching in parseOpArgs so a kebab-case flag maps to its camelCase op-schema key (accept --dry-run for dryRun, etc.), accepting both forms. Resolve as one flag-normalization layer together with FGAP-032 (the id-flag-specific instance). No op-schema change.

gaps.title: pi-context update merges a new block_kind config declaration but does not materialize its schema/starter — a dangling declaration until install
gaps.description: TASK-038's config-registry propagation additively merges a catalog-new block_kind entry into config.block_kinds. But a genuinely-new block_kind's schema file + starter block materialize ONLY from the installed_schemas / installed_blocks lists (string-array registries, not in TASK-038's four-registry scope), which updateContext does not propagate. So an additively-merged new block_kind is a dangling declaration: config declares the kind, but its schema is not copied and no starter block exists, until a subsequent install (which iterates installed_schemas/installed_blocks). relation_types / invariants / lenses have no such concern — they are fully functional as config declarations; only block_kinds couple to schema/block materialization.

evidence.reference: updateContext's TASK-038 registry-merge step merges block_kinds config entries; installContext materializes schemas/blocks only from config.installed_schemas / installed_blocks, not from block_kinds

mergeCatalogRegistries scope = the four keyed-array registries (relation_types/invariants/block_kinds/lenses); installed_schemas/installed_blocks (string-array) are out of scope, so a newly-declared block_kind's schema_path/data_path point at uncopied catalog files

gaps.impact: A catalog release that adds a new block_kind reaches an existing substrate (via update) as a config declaration without its schema/starter — the kind is declared but unusable (no schema to validate items against, no starter block) until a subsequent install. No data loss; the relation_types/invariants governance propagation (TASK-038's immediate driver) is unaffected.
gaps.canonical_vocabulary: block_kind materialization on update (schema + starter, not just declaration)
gaps.proposed_resolution: On update, for a block_kind additively merged into config, also propagate its installed_schemas / installed_blocks entries and materialize the schema + starter block via the install path, so a new block kind arrives complete (declaration + schema + starter); surfaced + previewed by --dryRun. Tracked as the materialization half of config-registry propagation (TASK-038 did the declaration half).

gaps.title: pi-context update --dryRun reports a version-bump schema as the would-act `resynced` set, not its precise migrate/block outcome — the preview can over-report
gaps.description: Under --dryRun, updateContext's catalog-ahead arm short-circuits BEFORE resyncSchema (no write) and reports the schema under `resynced` as the would-act set, because the concrete resynced/migrated/blocked outcome is unknowable without running the forward migration. So a version-bump schema that a live run would `migrate` (items forward-migrated) — or `block` (no safe chain, or migrated items fail revalidation) — is previewed as `resynced`. The would-register migration declarations (migrationsRegistered) are computed accurately read-only, but a live blocked run rolls those declarations back, so --dryRun can also list migrationsRegistered a live run would not keep. This undercuts FEAT-006's stated guarantee that --dry-run reports the full set of changes update would make: the per-schema OUTCOME under dryRun is optimistic, not exact, precisely when a schema is at risk of blocking.

evidence.reference: updateContext catalog-ahead dryRun branch pushes name onto result.resynced (the would-act set) and computes migrationsRegistered read-only; the live resynced/migrated/blocked classification and the block-path rollback of migrations.json are not modelled under dryRun

gaps.impact: A user previewing an update sees `resynced` for a schema a live run would actually migrate or refuse (block), and migrationsRegistered may list decls a blocked live run would roll back — the dry-run preview can over-state what an update would do, exactly for the schemas most at risk.
gaps.proposed_resolution: Under --dryRun compute the precise per-schema version-bump outcome READ-ONLY (find the catalog migration chain, forward-migrate the populated block in memory, AJV-validate against the new schema) and classify resynced vs migrated vs blocked — reporting migrationsRegistered only for non-blocked outcomes — without writing, so the preview matches a live run.

gaps.title: pi-context update does not reconcile a catalog registry entry whose body changed against a user-modified entry of the same id (additive-only) — the config-registry analog of the schema 3-way merge
gaps.description: TASK-038/FGAP-060's config-registry propagation is additive-only: an ABSENT catalog entry is appended, but an entry whose identity (canonical_id for relation_types/block_kinds, id for invariants/lenses) is already PRESENT is left exactly as the substrate has it — never reconciled — even when the catalog's body for that same id has changed. So a user who customizes a catalog registry entry's body never receives a later catalog change to that same entry (the user's version is kept entirely; the catalog's body change is silently dropped). This is the config-registry analog of FGAP-046 (the schema clobber/merge gap): there, schema BODIES get a 3-way merge preserving non-conflicting edits; here, config-registry entry bodies have NO merge — additive-only by design (mergeCatalogRegistries, context.ts).

evidence.reference: mergeCatalogRegistries appends only absent entries (by REGISTRY_DESCRIPTORS idField); a present id is never replaced or body-merged, even when the catalog body for that id diverges (additive-only)

gaps.impact: A user who tailors a catalog registry entry (e.g. a relation_type display_name/category, an invariant severity, a lens body) is frozen against future catalog changes to that entry — the value of a catalog registry update is capped for any customized entry, mirroring FGAP-046 for schemas but for config registries.
gaps.proposed_resolution: Extend update to reconcile a divergent catalog body against a user-modified registry entry of the same id via a 3-way merge (base = the as-installed catalog entry, ours = the substrate entry, theirs = the catalog entry), preserving non-conflicting customizations and surfacing conflicts — the registry analog of the FGAP-046 schema merge. Requires recording a per-registry-entry base (the registry equivalent of base-stamping).

gaps.title: the update conflict resolver spawns a subordinate `pi -p` mergetool instead of returning the conflict to the calling agent — the spawned agent's write-schema is auth-gate-refused (hasUI=false)
gaps.description: On a both-diverged schema CONFLICT, the CLI resolver (resolve-conflicts.ts) dispatches a subordinate pi-bound mergetool via runPiBound(['-p', prompt], …). `pi -p` runs pi NON-interactively (hasUI=false), and write-schema is auth-gated requiring hasUI=true, so the spawned agent's write is REFUSED — it can only print the reconciled schema and tell the operator to run write-schema manually; the reconciliation is never auto-applied. Deeper mis-design: the pi-context CLI is an agent-invoked surface (run by Claude Code or another shell-LLM, rarely a bare-shell human), so the CALLING agent already holds the conflict (the CLI's JSON `conflicts`) + the operator's authority + is interactive — spawning a SUBORDINATE agent to do the reconciliation is the wrong abstraction. DEC-0017's resolver route (a pi-bound agent mergetool that writes via write-schema under the auth-gate) was built for the rare human-at-bare-shell model and is internally inconsistent: the `-p` dispatch makes the session non-interactive, which DEC-0017's own R2 rejection already noted the auth-gate refuses. Surfaced by the live credentialed smoke; TASK-037's structural fake-spawn verification missed it (the fake spawn never ran real `pi -p` against the real auth-gate).

evidence.reference: runPiBound(['-p', prompt], …) dispatches the subordinate pi-bound mergetool; -p is pi's non-interactive print mode → the spawned session is hasUI=false
write-schema is authGated; the auth-gate refuses when hasUI===false, so the spawned agent's reconciled write is refused

live smoke repro: pi-context update --cwd <both-diverged-conflict substrate> → the real agent reconciles but 'write-schema requires interactive user confirmation … ctx.hasUI=false'; rebuild the substrate via /tmp/resolver-smoke-setup.mjs (installContext + a same-node tasks conflict)

gaps.impact: The agent-mergetool resolver never auto-applies a reconciliation. It degrades to: the agent prints the reconciled schema + the operator manually runs write-schema in an interactive pi. The conflict is surfaced and resolvable (never-clobber holds), but the one-command resolver UX is broken for its target case; and for the dominant agent-invoked usage the subordinate spawn is redundant — the calling agent should reconcile + apply.
gaps.canonical_vocabulary: caller-as-reconciler (no subordinate-agent spawn for an agent-invoked CLI)
gaps.proposed_resolution: Drop the pi-bound subordinate spawn from the resolver. update returns the typed conflict set (the rendered report + the structured `conflicts`); the calling agent (Claude Code / a shell-LLM) reconciles and applies the result via write-schema under the operator's confirm. Net-simpler code. Amends DEC-0017's resolver route. Re-verify with the live smoke (the calling agent applies the reconciliation end-to-end).

gaps.title: a calling agent cannot resolve an update conflict — write-schema does not advance the merge base, so update re-flags a reconciled schema forever
gaps.description: After the FGAP-068 rework, `pi-context update` surfaces a both-diverged schema CONFLICT and the report guides the calling agent to reconcile via write-schema. But following that guidance does NOT resolve the conflict: write-schema writes the resolved body (R) but never advances the merge BASE (config.installed_from.assets[name]), so the next update re-derives the identical 3-way base/ours/theirs disagreement — confirmed empirically (write-schema --yes of the resolved schema, then update --dryRun → the same conflict). The only value that converges is reconciling to the catalog's exact value (ours==theirs). The old (now-deleted) resolver called refreshBaselineForSchema after the agent's write; the rework dropped that with no replacement, leaving the calling agent with no working path to resolve a conflict. Correct semantics (Explore-verified against mergeSchema/checkStatus): a reconciliation means the agent has seen the catalog value (theirs) and chosen R; the new merge base must become the CATALOG body, so checkStatus reads locally-modified (catalogDrift false) and mergeSchema's base==theirs->ours rule (schema-merge.ts:168) takes R on the next update with zero conflicts, R preserved, re-conflicting only when the catalog later changes that path.

evidence.reference: updateContext conflict arm pushes to result.conflicts with NO baseline advance; the brought_current loop (refreshBaselineForSchema) runs only for resynced/migrated/merged, never conflicts — so the base stays stale after the agent's write-schema
mergeSchema atomic rule: base==theirs -> ours; advancing base to the catalog body makes a reconciled schema auto-merge to R with zero conflicts on the next update

empirical repro: pi-context update --cwd <both-diverged substrate> reports the conflict; write-schema --yes the resolved schema; update --dryRun re-reports the identical conflict (base/ours/theirs unchanged)

gaps.impact: The caller-as-reconciler model surfaces conflicts but provides no way to resolve them — a reconciled schema is re-flagged on every update unless the agent happens to pick the catalog's exact value. The conflict-handling guarantee of FEAT-006/update is not met end-to-end without a reconciliation-commit.
gaps.canonical_vocabulary: reconciliation-commit (advance merge base to catalog on conflict resolution)
gaps.proposed_resolution: Add a reflected, auth-gated commit op `resolve-conflict --schemaName <name> [--schema <R>]` that (i) writes R via writeSchemaCheckedForDir if provided, then (ii) advances installed_from.assets[name] (+ object-store stamp) to the CATALOG body (resolveCatalog -> samples/<schema_path>) via a stampBaselineFromBody helper factored from refreshBaselineForSchema. The conflict report's guidance names it. Then update auto-merges the reconciled schema (base==theirs->ours), zero conflicts.

gaps.title: update re-baselines an auto-merged schema to the merged body, not the catalog — so a kept-local divergence is resynced to catalog (clobbered) on the NEXT update
gaps.description: updateContext's post-merge baseline refresh (the brought_current set = resynced ∪ migrated ∪ merged, looped through refreshBaselineForSchema) stamps the baseline from the schema's ON-DISK body. For a 3-way merge that KEPT a local divergence (installed=merged=R, R≠catalog), this stamps base:=R. checkStatus then reads catalog-ahead (catalogDrift = catalog≠base true; installedDrift = installed=R≠base=R false), and the next update's catalog-ahead arm RESYNCS — copyFileSync of the catalog over the installed file — clobbering R. So a kept-local value survives exactly ONE update then is overwritten by catalog on the second. Empirically confirmed via live CLI: resolve-conflict (R=boolean, catalog=string) → update#1 merged, notes.type stays boolean → update#2 resynced, notes.type FLIPS to string → update#3 in-sync string. The class is GENERAL — every path landing a local divergence in result.merged is hit, INCLUDING the plain T3 disjoint auto-merge (the 'both the user's and the catalog's changes survive' guarantee is therefore also only-one-update-durable, not durable). Surfaced through the resolve-conflict (FGAP-069) multi-update demo; the defect is in the FEAT-006 3-way merge baseline (TASK-036), not in resolve-conflict (which stamps base:=catalog correctly). The convergence tests + tests (h)/(i) currently ASSERT the buggy post-merge catalog-ahead state, so the regression ships green.

evidence.reference: brought_current includes result.merged; the post-loop refreshBaselineForSchema (~:1371-1372) stamps from the on-disk merged body (~:1478), making a kept-local merge read catalog-ahead next run
checkStatus catalog-ahead derivation (catalogDrift && !installedDrift); the catalog-ahead arm resyncs via resyncSchema copyFileSync (~:529), overwriting the local divergence
the convergence rule base==theirs->ours: stamping base:=catalog instead makes the next update's merge take R (locally-modified), preserving it durably
test (h) asserts post-merge state catalog-ahead + baseline==merged-body hash — encodes the bug; test (i) ~:1046-1050 likewise; both stop after one update so the clobber is never exercised

gaps.impact: update's never-clobber guarantee fails on the SECOND update: both a reconciled conflict value and a plain disjoint local edit are silently overwritten by the catalog on the next update. The merge 'both survive' / reconciliation 'preserved' guarantees are only-one-update-durable.
gaps.canonical_vocabulary: post-merge baseline := catalog (theirs), not the merged body
gaps.proposed_resolution: In updateContext's merge arm, on the conflict-free branch, stamp base := the CATALOG (theirs) body via stampBaselineFromBody (theirs + sourceFile already in scope), !dryRun-guarded; remove result.merged from the post-loop on-disk refreshBaselineForSchema set (resynced/migrated keep it — their on-disk body IS catalog). Then checkStatus reads locally-modified, the next merge takes base==theirs->ours=R, fixed point installed=R/base=catalog stable across updates. Correct tests (h)/(i)/(d)/(j) to assert locally-modified + catalog baseline + multi-update durability.
